meta.title: cmux — The terminal built for multitasking
meta.description: Native macOS terminal built on Ghostty. Works with Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Kiro, Aider, and any CLI tool. Vertical tabs, notification rings, split panes, and a socket API.
meta.ogDescription: Native macOS terminal for AI coding agents. Works with Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Kiro, Aider, and any CLI tool.

home.subtitle: Native macOS app built on Ghostty. Vertical tabs, notification rings when agents need attention, split panes, and a <cliLink>CLI</cliLink> for programmability.

feature.verticalTabsDesc: : sidebar shows git branch, working directory, ports, and notification text
feature.notificationRingsDesc: : panes light up when agents need attention
feature.inAppBrowserDesc: : split a browser alongside your terminal with a scriptable API
feature.splitPanesDesc: : horizontal and vertical splits within each tab
feature.scriptableDesc: : CLI and socket API for automation and scripting
feature.gpuAcceleratedDesc: : powered by libghostty for smooth rendering
feature.keyboardShortcutsDesc: : <link>extensive shortcuts</link> for workspaces, splits, browser, and more

home.faqGhosttyQ: How does cmux relate to Ghostty?
home.faqGhosttyA: cmux is not a fork of Ghostty. It uses <link>libghostty</link> as a library for terminal rendering, the same way apps use WebKit for web views. Ghostty is a standalone terminal; cmux is a different app built on top of its rendering engine.
home.faqPlatformA: macOS only, for now. cmux is a native Swift + AppKit app.
home.faqAgentsQ: What coding agents does cmux work with?
home.faqAgentsA: All of them. cmux is a terminal, so any agent that runs in a terminal works out of the box: Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Kiro, Aider, Goose, Amp, Cline, Cursor Agent, and anything else you can launch from the command line.
home.faqNotificationsA: When a process needs attention, cmux shows notification rings around panes, unread badges in the sidebar, a notification popover, and a macOS desktop notification. These fire automatically via standard terminal escape sequences (OSC 9/99/777), or you can trigger them with the <cliLink>cmux CLI</cliLink> and <hooksLink>Claude Code hooks</hooksLink>.
home.faqShortcutsA: Terminal keybindings are read from your Ghostty config file (<configPath>~/.config/ghostty/config</configPath>). cmux-specific shortcuts (workspaces, splits, browser, notifications) can be customized in Settings. See the <link>default shortcuts</link> for a full list.
home.faqTmuxQ: How does it compare to tmux?
home.faqTmuxA: tmux is a terminal multiplexer that runs inside any terminal. cmux is a native macOS app with a GUI: vertical tabs, split panes, an embedded browser, and a socket API are all built in. No config files or prefix keys needed.
home.faqFreeA: Yes, cmux is free to use. The source code is available on <link>GitHub</link>.

passkeyAuth.title: Passkey auth in the cmux browser
passkeyAuth.summary: cmux's embedded browser supports passkey authentication and can import cookies from other browsers with cmux browser import.
passkeyAuth.p1: cmux's browser now supports passkey authentication. Sign-in flows that depend on passkeys, WebAuthn, Touch ID, or hardware security keys can complete inside a cmux browser pane.
passkeyAuth.p2: If you already use another browser for the same site, import compatible cookies once:
passkeyAuth.p3: Requires cmux v0.64+. This helps when Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, or another agent needs to test an authenticated local app without leaving cmux.

taskManager.title: Task Manager in cmux
taskManager.summary: Use cmux top or Task Manager from the command palette to see CPU and RAM usage for your coding agents.
taskManager.p1: cmux now has a task manager for checking how much CPU and RAM your coding agents and terminal processes are using.
taskManager.p3: Or press Cmd+Shift+P and choose Task Manager from the command palette.

sessionRestore.title: Session restore in cmux
sessionRestore.summary: How cmux restores layouts and resumes Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Antigravity CLI, Grok Build CLI, and other agent sessions.
sessionRestore.p1: Terminal workflows survive interruptions better when the app can reconstruct the shape of your work. cmux now treats the workspace layout as durable state instead of something tied to one app process.
sessionRestore.p2: The important boundary is live process state. cmux restores what it owns and what supported tools expose through their own resume APIs. It does not checkpoint arbitrary terminal processes.
sessionRestore.seoP: If you are looking for how to restore Claude Code, OpenCode, opencode, Codex, Gemini CLI, Antigravity CLI, Grok Build CLI, Amp, Cursor CLI, Rovo Dev, Copilot, CodeBuddy, Factory, Qoder, or Hermes Agent sessions after a terminal crash, install cmux hooks and keep agent resume enabled.
sessionRestore.baselineP: After a normal relaunch, cmux restores the app-level session snapshot:
sessionRestore.agentP: Claude Code, Codex, Grok Build CLI, OpenCode, Pi, Amp, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, Antigravity CLI, Rovo Dev, Hermes Agent, Copilot, CodeBuddy, Factory, and Qoder can resume when cmux has a native session ID. For most agents, install the integration with cmux hooks setup.
sessionRestore.agentP2: The setup command installs supported agents whose binaries are on PATH and skips the rest. Claude Code is handled by the cmux Claude wrapper when Claude integration is enabled in Settings.
sessionRestore.implementationP1: cmux writes a JSON session snapshot under Application Support with the window tree, workspace metadata, pane layout, terminal cwd, scrollback replay data, and browser navigation state.
sessionRestore.implementationP2: Agent hooks write session mappings under ~/.cmuxterm. On restore, cmux rebuilds the UI first. If automatic agent resume is enabled, it launches each supported agent with that agent's native resume command and the saved session ID.
sessionRestore.limitsP: tmux, vim, shells, and unsupported tools reopen as normal terminals unless they have a cmux integration that records a safe native resume command. That keeps restore predictable and avoids replaying stale prompts or secrets.
sessionRestore.docsCta: Read the <link>session restore docs</link> for setup commands, supported agents, and troubleshooting.

cmuxClaudeTeams.title: Claude Code teammate agents as native cmux panes
cmuxClaudeTeams.summary: Claude Code's teammate mode requires tmux. cmux fakes it so teammates become native splits with sidebar metadata and notifications.
cmuxClaudeTeams.p1: Claude Code has an experimental teammate mode that spawns sub-agents in parallel. It requires tmux and setting CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1. cmux claude-teams handles all of that for you: it sets the env var, places a tmux shim on PATH that translates every tmux command (split-window, send-keys, capture-pane) into cmux's native split/workspace API, and execs into claude --teammate-mode auto. One command.
cmuxClaudeTeams.p2: The shim translates split-window into surface.split, send-keys into surface.send_text, capture-pane into surface.read_text. Teammates stack vertically in a right column, auto-equalizing as agents spawn and exit. Every pane shows up in the sidebar with notifications. Works over SSH via the Go relay daemon.
cmuxClaudeTeams.p3: The same shim powers cmux omo for oh-my-openagent (formerly oh-my-opencode).

cmuxOmo.title: oh-my-openagent subagents as native cmux panes
cmuxOmo.summary: oh-my-openagent (formerly oh-my-opencode) orchestrates parallel specialist agents across Claude, GPT, and Gemini. cmux omo turns their tmux panes into native splits.
cmuxOmo.p1: cmux omo integrates oh-my-openagent (formerly oh-my-opencode), a plugin for OpenCode that orchestrates specialist agents across Claude, GPT, and Gemini in parallel. Each agent gets its own tmux pane. cmux omo uses the same tmux shim as cmux claude-teams: fake TMUX env var, every tmux command translated to cmux splits. It auto-installs the plugin into a shadow config so ~/.config/opencode stays untouched.
cmuxOmo.p2: The shim also intercepts terminal-notifier calls and routes them through cmux notify. Works over SSH via the Go relay daemon.

gpl.title: cmux is now GPL
gpl.summary: cmux relicensed from AGPL-3.0 to GPL-3.0.
gpl.p1: cmux relicensed from AGPL-3.0 to GPL-3.0. AGPL's network-use clause is irrelevant for a desktop app, but many companies ban AGPL entirely. GPL drops that clause while still requiring forks to stay open source. Dual-license (GPL + commercial) stays the same.

cmuxSsh.title: cmux SSH
cmuxSsh.summary: One command gives you persistent remote sessions, browser panes that reach remote ports, and agent notifications that come home.
cmuxSsh.p1: cmux ssh user@remote creates a workspace for the remote machine. Drag an image into a remote Claude Code session and it gets uploaded automatically. Browser panes route through the remote network, so localhost just works. Uses your ~/.ssh/config, reconnects on drops.
cmuxSsh.features: Browser panes route traffic through the remote network, so localhost:3000 reaches the remote dev server without -L flags. Coding agents on the remote box send notifications to your local sidebar. cmux claude-teams and cmux omo work over SSH, spawning teammate panes locally while computation runs remote. Drag a file into a remote terminal to upload via scp. The sidebar shows connection state and detected listening ports.

cmdShiftU.title: Cmd+Shift+U
cmdShiftU.summary: How Cmd+Shift+U navigates between finished agents across workspaces in cmux.
cmdShiftU.p1: My favorite cmux feature is Cmd+Shift+U. I have 17 workspaces open right now, each running an agent. I used to click through tabs and the notification panel to figure out what completed. Typing is faster.
cmdShiftU.p2: Cmd+Shift+U jumps to the newest unread <link>notification</link>. In practice that means the last agent that finished. It switches to the right workspace, focuses the exact pane, flashes it so you see where to look, and marks it read. If the notification came from another window, that window comes forward.

zenOfCmux.title: The Zen of cmux
zenOfCmux.summary: cmux is a primitive, not a solution. It gives you composable pieces and your workflow is up to you.
zenOfCmux.p1: cmux is not prescriptive about how developers hold their tools. It's a terminal and browser with a CLI, and the rest is up to you.
zenOfCmux.p2: cmux is a primitive, not a solution. It gives you a terminal, a browser, notifications, workspaces, splits, tabs, and a CLI to control all of it. cmux doesn't force you into an opinionated way to use coding agents. What you build with the primitives is yours.
zenOfCmux.p3: The best developers have always built their own tools. Nobody has figured out the best way to work with agents yet, and the teams building closed products definitely haven't either. The developers closest to their own codebases will figure it out first.
zenOfCmux.p4: Give a million developers composable primitives and they'll collectively find the most efficient workflows faster than any product team could design top-down.

showHnLaunch.title: Launching cmux on Show HN
showHnLaunch.summary: cmux hit #2 on Hacker News, got shared by Mitchell Hashimoto, and went viral in Japan.
showHnLaunch.intro: We posted cmux on <link>Show HN</link> on Feb 19:
showHnLaunch.blockquote1: I run a lot of Claude Code and Codex sessions in parallel. I was using Ghostty with a bunch of split panes, and relying on native macOS notifications to know when an agent needed me. But Claude Code's notification body is always just "Claude is waiting for your input" with no context, and with enough tabs open, I couldn't even read the titles anymore.
showHnLaunch.blockquote2: I tried a few coding orchestrators but most of them were Electron/Tauri apps and the performance bugged me. I also just prefer the terminal since GUI orchestrators lock you into their workflow. So I built cmux as a native macOS app in Swift/AppKit. It uses libghostty for terminal rendering and reads your existing Ghostty config for themes, fonts, colors, and more.
showHnLaunch.blockquote3: The main additions are the sidebar and notification system. The sidebar has vertical tabs that show git branch, working directory, listening ports, and the latest notification text for each workspace. The notification system picks up terminal sequences (OSC 9/99/777) and has a CLI (cmux notify) you can wire into agent hooks for Claude Code, OpenCode, etc. When an agent is waiting, its pane gets a blue ring and the tab lights up in the sidebar, so I can tell which one needs me across splits and tabs. Cmd+Shift+U jumps to the most recent unread.
showHnLaunch.blockquote4: The in-app browser has a scriptable API. Agents can snapshot the accessibility tree, get element refs, click, fill forms, evaluate JS, and read console logs. You can split a browser pane next to your terminal and have Claude Code interact with your dev server directly.
showHnLaunch.blockquote5: Everything is scriptable through the CLI and socket API: create workspaces/tabs, split panes, send keystrokes, open URLs in the browser.
showHnLaunch.hitNumber2: At peak it hit #2 on Hacker News. Mitchell Hashimoto shared it:
showHnLaunch.favoriteComment: My favorite comment from the <link>HN thread</link>:
showHnLaunch.viralJapan: Surprisingly, cmux went viral in Japan:
showHnLaunch.translation: Translation: "This looks good. A Ghostty-based terminal app designed so you don't get lost running multiple CLIs like Claude Code in parallel. The waiting-for-input panel gets a blue frame, and it has its own notification system."
showHnLaunch.extensions: Another exciting thing was seeing people build on top of the cmux CLI. sasha built a pi-cmux extension that shows model info, token usage, and agent state in the sidebar:
showHnLaunch.scriptable: Everything in cmux is scriptable through the CLI: creating workspaces, sending keystrokes, controlling the browser, reading notifications. Part of the cmux philosophy is being programmable and composable, so people can customize the way they work with coding agents. The state of the art for coding agents is changing fast, and you don't want to be locked into an inflexible GUI orchestrator that can't keep up.
showHnLaunch.cta: If you're running multiple coding agents, <link>give cmux a try</link>.

introducingCmux.title: Introducing cmux
introducingCmux.summary: A native macOS terminal built on Ghostty, designed for running multiple AI coding agents side by side.
introducingCmux.p1: cmux is a native macOS terminal application built on top of Ghostty, designed from the ground up for developers who run multiple AI coding agents simultaneously.
introducingCmux.whyP: Modern development workflows often involve running several agents at once. Claude Code, Codex, and other tools each in their own terminal. Keeping track of which ones need attention and switching between them quickly is the problem cmux solves.
introducingCmux.featureVerticalTabsDesc: see all your terminals at a glance in a sidebar
introducingCmux.featureNotificationsDesc: tabs flash when an agent needs your input
introducingCmux.featureSplitPanesDesc: horizontal and vertical splits within each workspace
introducingCmux.featureSocketApiDesc: programmatic control for creating tabs and sending input
introducingCmux.featureGpuDesc: powered by libghostty for smooth rendering
introducingCmux.getStartedP: Install cmux via Homebrew or download the DMG from the <link>getting started guide</link>.

cmuxFinder.title: cmux Finder
cmuxFinder.summary: cmux now has a file explorer that previews videos, images, PDFs, and markdown files.
cmuxFinder.p1: cmux now has a Finder-style file explorer in the sidebar.
cmuxFinder.p2: It previews videos, images, PDFs, and markdown files inside cmux, so you can inspect generated artifacts without leaving the workspace.
cmuxFinder.p3: I use it most for demo videos and screenshots that Codex records while it is testing changes.
cmuxFinder.p4: Toggle file previews from Cmd+Shift+P, then choose Enable Cmd-click File Previews or Disable Cmd-click File Previews.

cmuxVault.title: cmux Vault
cmuxVault.summary: Search Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, and Pi sessions from the Vault pane and drag them into your workspace.
cmuxVault.p1: cmux now has a Vault pane in the right sidebar for old agent sessions.
cmuxVault.p2: It indexes Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, and Pi sessions, with full-text search across transcripts.
cmuxVault.p3: Drag a session from Vault into a workspace to reopen it where you are working.

markdownViewer.title: A better markdown viewer in cmux
markdownViewer.summary: Open README.md with cmux open or drag markdown files from the right sidebar.
markdownViewer.p1: cmux has a better markdown viewer for README files, docs, and generated reports.
markdownViewer.p2: Open a file from the CLI:
markdownViewer.p3: You can also drag a markdown file from the right sidebar into the workspace.

unreadShortcuts.title: Unread workspace shortcuts in cmux
unreadShortcuts.summary: Cmd+Control+U cycles through unread workspaces while keeping them unread, and Cmd+Option+U toggles read state.
unreadShortcuts.p1: cmux has two more shortcuts for managing unread workspaces when agents finish at the same time.
unreadShortcuts.p2: Cmd+Control+U marks the current workspace as the oldest unread workspace, then jumps to the most recent unread workspace.
unreadShortcuts.p3: That sends the current workspace to the back of the unread cycle, so you can keep cycling through unread work without clearing it.
unreadShortcuts.p4: Cmd+Option+U toggles the current workspace read or unread.
unreadShortcuts.p5: This is useful when you jump to a new unread workspace with Cmd+Shift+U but need to context switch away before responding.

cmuxHistory.title: cmux history
cmuxHistory.summary: Reopen closed terminals, browsers, workspaces, and agent sessions with Cmd+Shift+T, and retrace your focus with Cmd+[ and Cmd+].
cmuxHistory.p1: You close a terminal tab to clear clutter, then realize you needed it. Or you close a whole workspace and lose the agent that was mid task. cmux now keeps a history of what you closed and what you focused, so getting it back takes one shortcut.
cmuxHistory.reopenP: Press Cmd+Shift+T to reopen the last thing you closed. It works for terminal tabs, browser tabs, workspaces, and windows, and restores them one at a time in the order you closed them. Each surface comes back where it was, with its panes intact.
cmuxHistory.agentP: History also reopens closed agent sessions. Reopen a Claude Code, Codex, or OpenCode session and it resumes where it left off. Run cmux hooks setup once so cmux can track agent sessions, then they reopen like everything else.
cmuxHistory.agentP2: This builds on cmux <link>session restore</link>, which resumes supported agents through their own native resume commands.
cmuxHistory.focusTitle: Jump back to where you were
cmuxHistory.focusP: The back and forward buttons in the titlebar, or Cmd+[ and Cmd+], jump to the previous and next workspace or window you were working in, the same way back and forward work in a browser.
cmuxHistory.fullHistoryP: When you need more than the last item, open the full History pane: a searchable, day grouped view of everything you closed and focused, with timestamps and a Clear Closed action.
cmuxHistory.docsCta: See the <link>keyboard shortcuts</link> for the full list, including how to rebind history navigation.

cmuxFinder.metaDescription: cmux v0.64+ includes a Finder-style file explorer that previews videos, images, PDFs, and markdown files inside your workspace.

cmuxVault.metaDescription: cmux v0.64+ adds a Vault pane for searching Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, and Pi sessions, then dragging them into your workspace.

markdownViewer.metaDescription: Open README.md with cmux open or drag markdown files from the right sidebar into the improved cmux markdown viewer.

unreadShortcuts.metaDescription: Use Cmd+Control+U and Cmd+Option+U to cycle through unread cmux workspaces without clearing unread state before you respond.

cmuxHistory.metaTitle: cmux history: reopen closed terminals and sessions
cmuxHistory.metaDescription: cmux keeps a history of what you closed and focused. Reopen closed terminals, browsers, workspaces, and Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode sessions with Cmd+Shift+T, and move focus back and forward with Cmd+[ and Cmd+].

remoteTmux.title: Remote tmux
remoteTmux.metaDescription: Drive a remote tmux session from cmux over SSH using tmux control mode. Sessions become workspaces, windows become tabs, and a window's panes become a native cmux split.
remoteTmux.intro: Remote tmux mirrors a tmux session running on a remote machine into cmux over SSH, using tmux control mode (tmux -CC). Instead of a plain SSH terminal, the remote session is reprojected into cmux's native UI — workspaces, tabs, splits, scrollback, and copy — and cmux drives the real tmux server behind it. It is a Beta Features flag and does not change local terminals.
remoteTmux.betaNote: Beta. This feature is opt-in and still stabilizing — see the limitations below.
remoteTmux.mappingIntro: cmux and tmux nest their layouts in opposite directions. In cmux, a pane holds a row of tabs (each tab is one terminal). In tmux, a window holds a split of panes. Remote tmux bridges the two by projecting tmux's structure onto cmux's:
remoteTmux.rowSession: A dedicated workspace in the sidebar.
remoteTmux.rowPane: A pane in a native split inside that tab.
remoteTmux.mappingPanes: The new capability is panes inside a tab: a cmux tab used to hold a single terminal, but a tmux window with several panes is now rendered as a real cmux split layout within one tab — each remote pane is a native cmux terminal pane with the usual chrome. The bridge is two-way: splitting or closing a pane in that tab runs tmux split-window, and reordering the tabs reorders the tmux windows with swap-window.
remoteTmux.requirementsDesc: A reachable SSH host (cmux reads your ~/.ssh/config) with tmux installed. Control mode (tmux -CC) is a standard tmux feature, so no special build is needed; a current tmux is recommended for the full feature set. cmux attaches over an SSH ControlMaster connection, and the remote tmux server keeps running when you detach.
remoteTmux.enableDesc: Open Settings → Beta Features and turn on Remote tmux. The toggle is off by default, so nothing changes for local terminals until you opt in.
remoteTmux.attachIntro: Run cmux ssh-tmux <destination> in a terminal — an ~/.ssh/config alias or user@host. cmux opens a new window that mirrors that host's tmux sessions: each session becomes a workspace, each window a tab, and multi-pane windows become in-tab splits.
remoteTmux.attachSockets: The remote.tmux.* socket commands (below) give finer control — for example, remote.tmux.mirror mirrors a host's sessions into the current window's sidebar instead of opening a dedicated window.
remoteTmux.attachCli: Hosts that authenticate non-interactively (ssh-agent, or a key in ~/.ssh/config) attach with no prompt. If the host needs interactive authentication (a password, host-key confirmation, or MFA), cmux runs ssh inline in your terminal so you can authenticate, then opens the mirror window. Accepts --port and --identity.
remoteTmux.troubleshootDesc: The destination is handed to ssh as-is, so an alias must carry everything ssh needs to log in — most commonly User (without it, ssh tries your local username) and IdentityFile:
remoteTmux.troubleshootFallback: Or pass the user explicitly: cmux ssh-tmux dev@my-ssh-alias. If plain ssh <destination> doesn't log you in, cmux ssh-tmux <destination> won't either.
remoteTmux.howDesc: cmux spawns ssh … tmux -CC attach and parses the control-mode stream itself rather than relying on a built-in tmux viewer, so the protocol and %begin/%end command correlation are fully owned by cmux. Each remote pane renders into a dedicated terminal surface fed by tmux %output, and your input — keystrokes and mouse — is forwarded with tmux send-keys. The remote tmux server owns pane sizing and reflow; cmux keeps its surfaces in lock-step with it and never reflows locally.
remoteTmux.behaviorSize: Sizing: the remote client is resized to the rendered grid (refresh-client -C), so TUIs aren't stuck at tmux's default 80×24.
remoteTmux.behaviorSplit: Splits: splitting or closing a pane in a mirrored window is propagated to tmux with split-window, so the cmux layout and the tmux layout stay in sync. Programmatic splits (cmux new-split or surface.split over the socket) report accepted with no surface id — the new pane arrives asynchronously once tmux confirms the layout change. Requests carrying options the routed split cannot honor (startup command, working directory, divider position, left/up placement) are rejected up front, before the remote session is mutated.
remoteTmux.behaviorReorder: Reordering: drag-reordering the mirrored tabs reorders the tmux windows with swap-window.
remoteTmux.behaviorCwd: Working directory: the remote pane's current folder is tracked and shown on the tab.
remoteTmux.behaviorPaste: Paste & drop: pasted text and dropped images/files go through tmux paste-buffer -p, so a remote app (e.g. a coding agent) receives a real bracketed paste — an image arrives as [Image #N], not a local path.
remoteTmux.behaviorMouse: Mouse: clicks, scroll, and drag reach the remote app when it has mouse mode on. Hold Shift while dragging for a native cmux text selection/copy (the same as any terminal running a mouse-mode app).
remoteTmux.behaviorUnicode: Unicode-correct output: multi-byte characters are preserved even when tmux splits them across updates, so box-drawing and other wide content render cleanly.
remoteTmux.socketDesc: The feature is also driven by socket commands (gated on the Beta Features flag). host is an SSH destination or ~/.ssh/config alias; session is a tmux session name.
remoteTmux.methodSessions: List the tmux sessions on a host.
remoteTmux.methodAttach: Attach a control client to a session (create attaches-or-creates).
remoteTmux.methodMirror: Mirror every session on a host, each as its own workspace (windows become tabs).
remoteTmux.methodWindow: Open a dedicated window mirroring every session on a host (the cmux ssh-tmux entry point); returns the ssh command to run for interactive auth when the host needs it.
remoteTmux.methodDetach: Detach the control client; the remote session keeps running.
remoteTmux.methodState: Report the control client's observed state (diagnostics).
remoteTmux.socketSafetyDesc: A dash-prefixed host or identity file is rejected at the trust boundary as a defense against SSH option injection.
remoteTmux.limitReconnect: Transient SSH drops reconnect automatically: if the connection blips mid-session the mirror freezes and cmux retries with capped exponential backoff, re-seeding the panes when it resumes. The mirror only closes for good when the remote session itself ends, or when you close the mirror window or quit cmux — and it isn't restored on relaunch (re-attach with cmux ssh-tmux).
remoteTmux.limitPaste: Only single-line paste/drop (such as a file or image path) is delivered as a real bracketed paste via paste-buffer -p; multi-line text is sent as plain keystrokes, so a remote app won't treat it as one paste.
remoteTmux.limitCwd: Live working-directory updates use control-mode subscriptions (tmux 3.2+); on older tmux the tab shows the initial folder but doesn't update on cd.
remoteTmux.limitReflow: Primary-screen scrollback isn't re-wrapped on resize — cmux leaves reflow to tmux, so older lines can stay at the previous width until the app repaints. Full-screen TUIs, which redraw on resize, are unaffected.

gettingStarted.title: Getting Started
gettingStarted.metaDescription: Install cmux, the native macOS terminal for AI coding agents. Homebrew, DMG download, CLI setup, and auto-updates via Sparkle.
gettingStarted.intro: cmux is a lightweight, native macOS terminal built on Ghostty for managing multiple AI coding agents. It features vertical tabs, a notification panel, and a socket-based control API.
gettingStarted.dmgDesc: Open the .dmg and drag cmux to your Applications folder. cmux auto-updates via Sparkle, so you only need to download once.
gettingStarted.firstLaunchCallout: On first launch, macOS may ask you to confirm opening an app from an identified developer. Click Open to proceed.
gettingStarted.verifyDesc: Open cmux and you should see:
gettingStarted.verifyItem1: A terminal window with a vertical tab sidebar on the left
gettingStarted.verifyItem3: The Ghostty-powered terminal ready for input
gettingStarted.cliDesc: cmux includes a command-line tool for automation. Inside cmux terminals it works automatically. To use the CLI from outside cmux, create a symlink:
gettingStarted.cliThen: Then you can run commands like:
gettingStarted.autoUpdatesDesc: cmux checks for updates automatically via Sparkle. When an update is available you'll see an update pill in the titlebar. You can also check manually via cmux > Check for Updates in the menu bar.
gettingStarted.sessionRestoreDesc: cmux restores layout and metadata after relaunch. Supported agent sessions can also resume when hooks have captured a native resume token.
gettingStarted.sessionCallout: cmux does not checkpoint arbitrary live process state. Unsupported terminal apps such as tmux, vim, and ordinary shells reopen as normal terminals.
gettingStarted.sessionRestoreLink: See the <link>session restore guide</link> for agent hook setup and the supported resume matrix.

sessionRestore.title: Session Restore
sessionRestore.metaDescription: How cmux restores windows, workspaces, panes, working directories, scrollback, browser history, and Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Antigravity CLI, Grok Build CLI, and other agent sessions after relaunch.
sessionRestore.intro: cmux saves the shape of your work so relaunching the app can bring back the same windows, workspaces, panes, terminal context, and browser state.
sessionRestore.restoredDesc: After relaunch, cmux restores app-owned layout and metadata:
sessionRestore.liveProcessCallout: cmux does not checkpoint arbitrary live process state. tmux, vim, shells, and unsupported terminal apps reopen as normal terminals unless they have their own cmux resume integration.
sessionRestore.agentResumeDesc: Supported AI coding agents can resume when cmux has captured the agent's native session ID. Install hooks after installing the agent CLI so the agent binary is on PATH:
sessionRestore.setupBehavior: Running cmux hooks setup installs every supported integration it can find and prints a summary for skipped agents. Use an agent name when you only want one integration.
sessionRestore.surfaceBindingsDesc: Advanced users and integrations can bind any terminal surface to a restart command. cmux stores public CLI and socket-created bindings for inspection and manual restore unless you approve a signed command prefix.
sessionRestore.surfaceBindingsNote: Review or edit approved prefixes in Settings > Terminal > Resume Commands. cmux only auto-runs resume bindings it marks trusted, such as live process-detected tmux bindings or user-approved prefixes. cmux still does not checkpoint arbitrary process memory. Sensitive environment keys such as tokens, passwords, secrets, and API keys are dropped before a resume binding is stored. Approved prefixes are also bound to the working directory and exact environment values when present.
sessionRestore.supportedNote: Claude Code is handled by the cmux Claude wrapper when Claude integration is enabled in Settings. Antigravity also accepts agy as the setup alias, and Rovo Dev accepts rovo.
sessionRestore.manualDesc: cmux restores the last saved snapshot on normal launch. You can also reapply the previous snapshot manually:
sessionRestore.disableDesc: To restore panes without launching saved agent resume commands, turn off Settings > Terminal > Resume Agent Sessions on Reopen or set:
sessionRestore.disableNote: This only disables agent resume commands. cmux still restores layout, working directories, scrollback, and browser history.
sessionRestore.underHoodItemSnapshot: cmux writes a versioned JSON snapshot to ~/Library/Application Support/cmux/session-<bundle-id>.json, plus a previous-session cache for manual reopen.
sessionRestore.underHoodItemScrollback: Terminal scrollback is stored as bounded text and replayed through a temporary file on restore. This is best effort because terminal apps can redraw or clear their screen.
sessionRestore.underHoodItemHooks: Agent hooks write ~/.cmuxterm/<agent>-hook-sessions.json with the agent session ID, cmux workspace ID, surface ID, cwd, process ID when available, and a sanitized launch command.
sessionRestore.underHoodItemResume: On restore, cmux rebuilds windows and panes first. If automatic agent resume is enabled, it launches a one-shot shell command that runs the agent's native resume command with the saved session ID.
sessionRestore.underHoodMore: The regular <configLink>configuration docs</configLink> cover cmux.json. Session restore keeps app layout separate from Ghostty terminal rendering settings.

concepts.title: Concepts
concepts.metaDescription: How cmux organizes terminals: windows, workspaces, panes, and surfaces. The hierarchy behind the sidebar, splits, and socket API.
concepts.intro: cmux organizes your terminals in a four-level hierarchy. Understanding these levels helps when using the socket API, CLI, and keyboard shortcuts.
concepts.windowDesc: A macOS window. Open multiple windows with {shortcut}. Each window has its own sidebar with independent workspaces.
concepts.workspaceDesc: A sidebar entry. Each workspace contains one or more split panes. Workspaces are what you see listed in the left sidebar.
concepts.workspaceNote: In the UI and keyboard shortcuts, workspaces are often called "tabs" since they behave like tabs in the sidebar. The socket API and environment variables use the term "workspace".
concepts.paneDesc: A split region within a workspace. Created by splitting with {right} (right) or {down} (down). Navigate between panes with {nav} + arrow keys.
concepts.paneNote: Each pane can hold multiple surfaces (tabs within the pane).
concepts.surfaceDesc: A tab within a pane. Each pane has its own tab bar and can hold multiple surfaces. Created with {new}, navigated with {prev} / {next} or {jump}.
concepts.surfaceNote: Surfaces are the individual terminal or browser sessions you interact with. Each surface has its own CMUX_SURFACE_ID environment variable.
concepts.panelDesc: The content inside a surface. Currently two types:
concepts.panelNote: Panel is mostly an internal concept. In the socket API and CLI, you interact with surfaces rather than panels directly.
concepts.visualItem1: The window contains a sidebar with three workspaces (dev, server, logs)
concepts.visualItem2: Workspace "dev" is selected, showing two panes side by side
concepts.visualItem3: Pane 1 has two surfaces ([S1] and [S2] in the tab bar), with S1 active
concepts.visualItem5: Each surface contains a panel (a terminal in this case)
concepts.workspaceGroupsDesc: Group related workspaces into collapsible named sections in the sidebar. Each group is owned by an anchor workspace and can be pinned, renamed, and given its own icon and color. New workspaces can be spawned directly inside a group from its header.
concepts.workspaceGroupsLink: Read the full Workspace Groups guide

workspaceGroups.metaDescription: Nest workspaces into collapsible named sections in the cmux sidebar. The anchor model, how to create and manage a group, and the workspace-group CLI.
workspaceGroups.title: Workspace Groups
workspaceGroups.intro: Workspace groups let you nest workspaces into collapsible named sections in the sidebar. Each group has an implicit anchor workspace, a customizable + button for spawning new workspaces inside it, and right-click actions for renaming, pinning, ungrouping, and editing its configuration.
workspaceGroups.anchorDesc: Every group is owned by exactly one workspace called the anchor. The group header in the sidebar is the anchor's representation — there is no separate row for it. Clicking the header name area focuses the anchor's panels; clicking the chevron toggles collapse.
workspaceGroups.anchorNew: Anchors are always brand new when a group is created. They are never promoted from an existing workspace. The anchor's working directory is inherited from the first selected workspace when grouping a selection, or from the active workspace when creating via the CLI without --cwd.
workspaceGroups.anchorClose: Closing the anchor workspace dissolves the group: every other member becomes an ungrouped workspace and stays in the tabs list. Nothing is closed besides the anchor itself. The app shows a confirm dialog with a "Don't ask again" toggle before this happens.
workspaceGroups.identityDesc: A group has a name, an icon (an SF Symbol, default folder.fill), and an optional custom color. These are independent of the anchor workspace's own customizations. The anchor's color and icon are seeded from the group on creation, but they can diverge afterwards.
workspaceGroups.pinningDesc: Groups can be pinned independently of individual workspace pins. Pinned top-level rows, whether individual workspaces or groups, stay above unpinned rows. Within each tier, groups and workspaces keep the order you drag them into.
workspaceGroups.layoutIntro: The sidebar lays out top-level rows top to bottom:
workspaceGroups.creatingIntro: A group is created in one of two ways: a keyboard shortcut or a workspace context menu. Both insert a fresh anchor workspace above the selection and move the selected workspaces into the group. Once a group exists, you manage it and add workspaces to it from the group header (see Managing a group below).
workspaceGroups.keyboardDesc: Select two or more workspaces in the sidebar, then press {shortcut}. A fresh anchor workspace is inserted above the selection and all selected workspaces become children. The group is auto-named Group 1, Group 2, and so on — rename it anytime via the header context menu.
workspaceGroups.keyboardNote: {shortcut} collides with React Grab's default. The group handler only consumes the chord when there is an explicit sidebar multi-selection of at least two workspaces, so React Grab still fires in single-selection and in browser or terminal contexts. Rebind it in Settings → Keyboard if you would rather the two not share a key.
workspaceGroups.keyboardSingle: Single-tab groups are not created from the shortcut. Use the workspace context menu New Group from Workspace entry for that.
workspaceGroups.contextMenuDesc: Right-click any workspace in the sidebar and choose New Group from Workspace, or New Group from Selection when multiple workspaces are selected. This uses the same auto-naming behavior as the shortcut.
workspaceGroups.managingIntro: Once a group exists, its header context menu and the + button on the header let you manage the group and add workspaces to it. Neither creates a new group.
workspaceGroups.headerMenuTitle: From the group header context menu
workspaceGroups.headerMenuDesc: Right-click an existing group header for Rename Group…, Pin Group / Unpin Group, Edit Group Config… (which opens ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json), Open Workspace Groups Docs, Ungroup (Keep Workspaces), and Delete Group (Close Workspaces). Delete is destructive and prompts for confirmation; ungroup keeps the workspaces and just removes the container.
workspaceGroups.plusButtonTitle: From the + button on a group header
workspaceGroups.plusButtonDesc: Hover over a group header to reveal a trailing + button. Click it to create a new workspace in the group at the anchor working directory. Right-click it for New Workspace in Group, Edit Group Config…, and Open Workspace Groups Docs.
workspaceGroups.plusButtonNote: Pressing {shortcut} while the active workspace is a group anchor or member also creates the new workspace inside that group. The default placement is After current: from a regular group member the new workspace lands right after the active member, and from the anchor or header it lands at the top of the group.
workspaceGroups.cliDesc: All group operations are scriptable with the cmux workspace-group subcommands. The hyphenated form ships first; once the broader cmux workspace command namespace lands, cmux workspace group becomes the canonical form, with the hyphenated form kept as an alias forever.
workspaceGroups.cliCreateNote: create returns a group handle (workspace_group:N by default). Pass --json for the full structured payload.
workspaceGroups.cliFlagsNote: Pass set-color or set-icon an empty value to clear the group's color or icon. delete is irreversible: it closes every member workspace.
workspaceGroups.cliExampleGroup: Group the currently selected workspaces under a name:
workspaceGroups.cliExampleNew: Spin up a new workspace inside an existing group, for example wired to a worktree script:
workspaceGroups.cliExampleList: List groups in the focused window:
workspaceGroups.configNote: Per-group configuration lives under the workspaceGroups key in ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json, keyed by the anchor workspace's working directory. See the configuration reference for the supported keys, including the global new-workspace placement and per-directory color, icon, placement, and context-menu actions.
workspaceGroups.persistenceDesc: Group name, anchor, pin state, collapse state, color, and icon are saved alongside your workspaces and restored across launches. Group membership is stored on each workspace.

configuration.title: Configuration
configuration.metaDescription: Configure cmux via Ghostty config files and ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json. Terminal rendering stays in Ghostty config, while cmux app settings, shortcuts, automation, sidebar, notifications, and browser behavior live in cmux.json.
configuration.intro: cmux reads terminal configuration from Ghostty config files. cmux-owned app settings also live in ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json, including shortcuts, automation, sidebar behavior, notifications, and browser preferences.
configuration.configLocationsDesc: cmux looks for configuration in these locations (in order):
configuration.createConfig: Create the config file if it doesn't exist:
configuration.appSettingsDesc: In-app settings are available via cmux > Settings ({shortcut}):
configuration.automationCmux: cmux processes only: only allow processes started inside cmux terminals to connect
configuration.automationAll: allowAll: allow any local process to connect (CMUX_SOCKET_MODE=allowAll, env override only)
configuration.automationCallout: On shared machines, consider using "Off" or "cmux processes only" mode.
configuration.browserLinkDesc: In Settings > Browser, cmux exposes two host lists with different purposes:
configuration.browserHostsEmbed: Hosts to Open in Embedded Browser: applies to links clicked from terminal output. Hosts in this list open in cmux; other hosts open in your default browser. Supports one host or wildcard per line (for example: example.com, *.internal.example).
configuration.browserHostsHttp: HTTP Hosts Allowed in Embedded Browser: applies only to HTTP (non-HTTPS) URLs. Hosts in this list can open in cmux without a warning prompt. Defaults include localhost, *.localhost, 127.0.0.1, ::1, 0.0.0.0, and *.localtest.me.
configuration.settingsFileDesc: cmux.json stores cmux-owned app settings, shortcuts, actions, commands, and workspace layouts.
configuration.settingsFilePrecedence: Shortcuts defined in cmux.json override the in-app Settings value. Managed shortcuts stay visible in Settings but become read-only there.
configuration.settingsFileChords: Use a string for a single shortcut or a two-item array for a chord. Example: ["ctrl+b", "c"] means press Ctrl+B, then C.

automation.ampIntegration: Enable cmux integration hooks for Amp. When disabled, the bundled plugin stays inactive without needing to be removed.
automation.suppressSubagentNotifications: Suppress visible completion notifications and status mutations from nested Codex or Claude child agents while keeping their events in Feed telemetry.
automation.workspaceAutoNaming: Opt-in AI auto-naming of workspaces and tabs from agent conversation content. When enabled, cmux summarizes supported agent sessions into short titles using each agent's own binary; manual renames always win.
automation.autoNamingAgent: Which agent generates auto-names for every session. "auto" (default) names each session with its own agent; any agent slug (claude, codex, grok, opencode, pi, omp, …) overrides naming for all sessions, even other agents' sessions. Undriveable or uninstalled agents fall back to the session's own agent, so naming never breaks.

app.confirmQuit: Control when cmux asks for confirmation before quitting. DEV builds always quit immediately regardless of this setting. Legacy app.warnBeforeQuit is still accepted as a boolean fallback.
app.windowTitleTemplate: Optional NSWindow title template. Blank preserves cmux's existing default title behavior, including the current-directory fallback. Supported placeholders: '{windowId}', '{windowToken}', '{activeWorkspace}', '{activeDirectory}', '{defaultTitle}', '{appName}'.
app.warnBeforeQuit: Legacy boolean fallback for quit confirmation. Use app.confirmQuit for new configs.
app.warnBeforeClosingTabXButton: Show a confirmation before closing a tab with the tab close button.
app.hideTabCloseButton: Hide tab close buttons in the pane tab bar.
app.forkConversationDefaultDestination: Default destination for the tab context menu's primary Fork Conversation action. The submenu still exposes every destination.

terminal.copyOnSelect: Copy selected terminal text to the system clipboard when the selection is committed, including word and line selections from double- and triple-click.
terminal.showTextBoxOnNewTerminals: Show the beta TextBox input by default for newly created workspaces, terminal tabs, and terminal splits.
terminal.focusTextBoxOnNewTerminals: Focus the beta TextBox input by default for newly created workspaces, terminal tabs, and terminal splits. Focusing also shows the TextBox.

browser.defaultSearchEngine: Default search engine for non-URL browser address bar queries. Use custom with customSearchEngineURLTemplate for arbitrary providers.
browser.customSearchEngineName: Display name used when defaultSearchEngine is custom.
browser.customSearchEngineURLTemplate: Search URL used when defaultSearchEngine is custom. Include the query placeholder or %s for the encoded query. If omitted, cmux appends q= to the URL.

workspaceGroups.newWorkspacePlacement: Global default for where Cmd-N inside a group, the group header + button, and configured group actions place the new workspace: `afterCurrent` (after the active in-group workspace, falling back to top), `top` (second slot, just after the anchor), or `end` (after the last member).

byCwd.newWorkspacePlacement: Per-cwd override for where Cmd-N inside the group, the group header + button, and configured group actions place the new workspace: `afterCurrent` (after the active in-group workspace, falling back to top), `top` (second slot, just after the anchor), or `end` (after the last member). Falls back to the global default when omitted.

markdown.fontSize: Default body font size, in points, for newly opened markdown viewers. Zoom a viewer live with Cmd-+ / Cmd-- / Cmd-0.
markdown.fontFamily: Default body font family for newly opened markdown viewers. Leave empty for the system markdown font stack.
markdown.maxWidth: Default maximum reading column width, in CSS pixels, for newly opened markdown viewers.

canvas.paneGap: Spacing between panes in the canvas layout, in points. Snapping, tidy, and new-pane placement all use this one gap.
canvas.snappingEnabled: Snap pane drags and resizes to neighbor edges and the pane gap. Hold Command to suspend snapping for one gesture.

fileEditor.wordWrap: Wrap long lines at the editor's right edge instead of scrolling horizontally.

sidebar.rightMaxWidth: Maximum width in points for the right sidebar. When omitted, the built-in dynamic cap applies.

fileExplorer.doubleClickAction: What double-clicking (or pressing Return on a search result for) a file in the file explorer does. `preview` opens the built-in cmux file preview (the default and historical behavior). `defaultEditor` opens with the macOS default app for the file type. `preferredEditor` opens with the `app.preferredEditor` command, falling back to the default app when none is set. Only applies to files; directories always expand/collapse, and non-local (remote) file explorers always open the cmux preview.

diffViewer.defaultLayout: Default layout for newly opened diff viewers. The toolbar layout toggle persists the user's last choice; an explicit `cmux diff --layout` option overrides both.

shortcuts.when: Optional per-action context predicates (VS Code-style `when` clauses), keyed by cmux action id. Each value is a boolean expression over context keys combined with !, &&, ||, and parentheses. Boolean keys: sidebarFocus, browserFocus, markdownFocus, terminalFocus, commandPaletteVisible, terminalFindVisible. Typed keys support comparisons: the string sidebarMode (files, find, sessions, feed, or dock) and the integers paneCount and workspaceCount. Comparison operators are ==, !=, =~ (regex), <, <=, >, >=, and `in [a, b]`; an unknown or absent key reads as false. The boolean literals true and false are also accepted; `key == false` is the same as `!key`. The action's shortcut only fires (and only conflicts with other shortcuts) when the clause holds. Examples: { "selectWorkspaceByNumber": "!sidebarFocus" } selects workspaces with Ctrl+1–9 everywhere except when the right sidebar is focused; { "selectSurfaceByNumber": "sidebarMode == 'find' && paneCount > 1" } scopes a binding to the Find sidebar when the workspace has multiple panes.
shortcuts.showModifierHoldHints: Show shortcut-hint chips while holding Cmd or Control.

configuration.exampleMarkdownFontSize: Default body font size (points) for newly opened markdown viewers.
configuration.exampleMarkdownFontSizeZoom: Zoom a viewer live with Cmd-+ / Cmd-- / Cmd-0.
configuration.exampleMarkdownFontFamily: Default body font family. Empty keeps the system markdown font stack.
configuration.exampleMarkdownMaxWidth: Default maximum reading column width, in CSS pixels.
configuration.exampleFileEditorWordWrap: Wrap long lines at the editor's right edge instead of scrolling horizontally.
configuration.exampleFileExplorerDoubleClickAction: Double-click a file: preview (default), defaultEditor (macOS default app), or preferredEditor (the app.preferredEditor command).
configuration.shortcutsWhenIntro: Optional per-action context predicates (VS Code-style when clauses), keyed by cmux action id. A binding only fires — and only conflicts with another binding on the same keystroke — when its clause holds. Omit a clause to keep the action's built-in context. The clause vocabulary:
configuration.shortcutsWhenBooleanKeys: boolean keys. An unknown or absent key reads as false; the literals true and false are also accepted.
configuration.shortcutsWhenTypedKeys: typed keys for comparisons: the right sidebar's active mode (a string) plus pane and workspace counts (integers).
configuration.shortcutsWhenOperators: boolean operators, typed comparisons, regex match, and list membership. Comparisons bind tighter than && and ||.
configuration.shortcutsWhenExample: For example, this makes Ctrl+1–9 select workspaces everywhere except when the right sidebar is focused (leaving Ctrl+1–5 free for the sidebar's mode switcher), and scopes surface selection to the Find sidebar when the workspace has multiple panes:

customCommands.title: Custom Commands
customCommands.metaDescription: Define actions, custom commands, and workspace layouts in cmux.json. Supports per-project and global configuration with explicit reloads.
customCommands.intro: Define actions, custom commands, and workspace layouts by adding .cmux/cmux.json to your project or ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json globally. Actions can appear in the surface tab bar and Command Palette.
customCommands.fileLocationsDesc: cmux looks for configuration in two places:
customCommands.localConfigDesc: lives in your project directory, takes precedence
customCommands.nightlyFeatureCallout: The action registry is a nightly feature. Install the latest nightly build before using <actions>actions</actions>, <shortcut>shortcut</shortcut>, or <buttons>ui.surfaceTabBar.buttons</buttons>.
customCommands.trustCallout: Project-local actions show up in the surface tab bar and Command Palette immediately. The first run still prompts for trust. Trust is per exact action fingerprint, not per repo. Project-local image icons stay locked until that action is trusted.
customCommands.schemaErrorCallout: If a project or global config has a schema error, cmux falls back to the next valid config and shows a <title>cmux.json Schema Error</title> row in Command Palette. Select it to open the config file.
customCommands.globalConfigDesc: applies to all projects, fills in commands not defined locally
customCommands.precedenceNote: Local actions and commands override global entries with the same ID or name.
customCommands.liveReload: Edit cmux.json, then press Cmd+Shift+, or run cmux reload-config to apply changes.
customCommands.schemaDesc: A cmux.json file can contain an actions registry plus a commands array. Commands are either simple shell commands or full workspace definitions:
customCommands.schemaIntro: <commands>commands</commands> still define reusable shell commands and workspace layouts. Nightly builds add an <actions>actions</actions> registry. Actions are the public IDs shared by the surface tab bar, the Command Palette, and action-level shortcuts.
customCommands.nightlyActionRegistryDesc: <actions>actions</actions> maps stable IDs to runnable behavior. Use the built-in IDs <newTerminal>cmux.newTerminal</newTerminal>, <newBrowser>cmux.newBrowser</newBrowser>, <splitRight>cmux.splitRight</splitRight>, and <splitDown>cmux.splitDown</splitDown> to override the defaults. Use your own IDs for project-specific tools.
customCommands.paletteDesc: <palette>palette</palette> defaults to <trueValue>true</trueValue>. Set it to <falseValue>false</falseValue> to keep an action out of Command Palette while still making it available to the surface tab bar or a shortcut. <shortcut>shortcut</shortcut> uses the same syntax as settings shortcuts, for example <singleShortcut>cmd+shift+c</singleShortcut> or <chordShortcut>["cmd+k", "cmd+c"]</chordShortcut>.
customCommands.iconsDesc: <buttons>ui.surfaceTabBar.buttons</buttons> replaces the default button list when present. Leave out a built-in ID to hide it. Icons always use an object shape: <symbolIcon>{ "type": "symbol", "name": "play.circle" }</symbolIcon>, <emojiIcon>{ "type": "emoji", "value": "🧪", "scale": 0.9 }</emojiIcon>, or <imageIcon>{ "type": "image", "path": "./icons/codex.svg" }</imageIcon>. Image paths are relative to the config file. Emoji <scale>scale</scale> is optional and defaults to <defaultScale>1</defaultScale>. SVG, PDF, PNG, JPEG, GIF, TIFF, BMP, HEIC, HEIF, WebP, AVIF, ICO, and ICNS are supported.
customCommands.buttonEntriesDesc: Each button entry can be either an action ID string or a button object. Use a button object when you want the same action with a different surface label, icon, or tooltip. The resolved button title is also used as the trust prompt title.
customCommands.permissionFlagsDesc: Put any approval or permission flags directly in the command string you actually want to run. The default action target is <target>newTabInCurrentPane</target>, so the common pattern is to open a new terminal tab in the current pane and start Codex, Claude Code, or OpenCode there.
customCommands.customActionsDesc: An <actions>actions</actions> entry is the reusable thing cmux runs. Use actions when the same behavior should be available from the Command Palette, surface tab bar, shortcuts, or the plus-button menu. Keep <commands>commands</commands> for reusable shell commands and workspace layouts. Set <palette>palette</palette> to false when an action should stay out of Command Palette.
customCommands.actionTypeBuiltin: Alias a built-in cmux action such as cmux.newTerminal, cmux.newBrowser, cmux.splitRight, or cmux.splitDown.
customCommands.actionTypeCommand: Run shell text in a terminal. Use target to choose the current terminal or a new tab in the current pane.
customCommands.actionTypeAgent: Start a supported coding agent. Today this supports codex and claude, with optional args.
customCommands.actionTypeWorkspaceCommand: Run a named workspace definition from commands. Use this for multi-pane layouts, custom working directories, and startup commands.
customCommands.actionFieldTitle: Command Palette row title, surface button label, menu item title, and trust prompt title unless an entry overrides it.
customCommands.actionFieldSubtitle: Command Palette secondary text. description is accepted as an alias for subtitle.
customCommands.actionFieldKeywords: Extra search terms for Command Palette.
customCommands.actionFieldPalette: Defaults to true for custom actions. Set false to hide the action from Command Palette while keeping it callable elsewhere.
customCommands.actionFieldShortcut: Optional action shortcut, using the same single-stroke or two-stroke chord syntax as settings shortcuts.
customCommands.actionFieldTarget: For command and agent actions only. Use currentTerminal or newTabInCurrentPane.
customCommands.commandPaletteBehaviorDesc: Command Palette reads the resolved action registry. Custom action IDs are added as rows when <palette>palette</palette> is not false. Legacy <commands>commands</commands> are added automatically as custom rows unless an action with the same generated ID already exists. Built-in commands keep their normal palette labels, but overriding a built-in ID such as <newTerminal>cmux.newTerminal</newTerminal> changes the behavior behind that shared entrypoint.
customCommands.commandPaletteScreenshotAlt: cmux Command Palette showing a custom Codex Worktree action from cmux.json
customCommands.commandPaletteScreenshotCaption: Custom actions with palette enabled show up as searchable Command Palette rows.
customCommands.plusButtonScreenshotAlt: cmux plus button right-click menu showing Codex Worktree, New Terminal, and New Browser
customCommands.plusButtonScreenshotCaption: The right-click menu follows the order from ui.newWorkspace.contextMenu.
customCommands.newWorkspaceButtonDesc: Use <action>ui.newWorkspace.action</action> to override what the plus button does. Use <contextMenu>ui.newWorkspace.contextMenu</contextMenu> (or the <rightClick>rightClick</rightClick> alias) to define the ordered right-click menu. Menu entries can be action IDs, action objects, or <separator>{ "type": "separator" }</separator>.
customCommands.newWorkspaceWorktreeNote: This example makes the normal plus click run the <action>worktree-agents</action> action. The workspace command from <commands>commands</commands> uses a visible setup terminal to create the Git <worktree>worktree</worktree> first. Codex and Claude start at the same time, wait for the workspace-specific state file, then cd into the created directory before exec.
customCommands.simpleCommandsDesc: A simple command runs a shell command in the currently focused terminal:
customCommands.fieldKeywords: Extra search terms for the command palette
customCommands.fieldCommand: Shell command to run in the focused terminal
customCommands.fieldConfirm: Show a confirmation dialog before running
customCommands.simpleCommandCwdNote: Simple commands run in the focused terminal's current working directory. If your command relies on project-relative paths, prefix it with
customCommands.simpleCommandCwdRepoRoot: to run from the repo root, or
customCommands.workspaceCommandsDesc: A workspace command creates a new workspace with a custom layout of splits, terminals, and browser panes:
customCommands.wsFieldName: Workspace tab name (defaults to command name)
customCommands.wsFieldLayout: Layout tree defining splits and panes
customCommands.restartBehaviorDesc: Controls what happens when a workspace with the same name already exists:
customCommands.layoutTreeDesc: The layout tree defines how panes are arranged using recursive split nodes:
customCommands.splitChildren: Exactly two child nodes (split or pane)
customCommands.paneNodeDesc: A leaf node containing one or more surfaces (tabs within the pane).
customCommands.surfaceDefinitionDesc: Each surface in a pane can be a terminal or a browser:
customCommands.surfaceCommand: Shell command to auto-run on creation (terminal only)

keyboardShortcuts.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
keyboardShortcuts.description: Default cmux keyboard shortcuts. Every cmux-owned shortcut can be changed in Settings or ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json, including two-step chords.
keyboardShortcuts.metaDescription: Default cmux keyboard shortcuts for workspaces, surfaces, split panes, browser, notifications, find, and app controls on macOS. All cmux-owned shortcuts are configurable in Settings or cmux.json.
keyboardShortcuts.chordsIntro: cmux supports two-step shortcut chords in <settingsFile>~/.config/cmux/cmux.json</settingsFile>. For the full configuration schema, see the <configurationLink>configuration docs</configurationLink>.
keyboardShortcuts.chordsCallout: Settings can edit shortcuts directly. Use cmux.json when you want an exact tmux-style prefix binding, keep shortcuts in dotfiles, or unbind an action with null, an empty string, "none", "clear", "unbound", or "disabled".
keyboardShortcuts.chordsRuleSingle: Use a plain string for a one-step shortcut.
keyboardShortcuts.chordsRuleArray: Use a two-item array for a chord. The first item is the prefix stroke, the second is the key that follows it.
keyboardShortcuts.chordsRuleSyntax: Each item uses the same syntax as regular bindings, for example cmd+b, ctrl+b, shift+/, or ctrl+1.

cat.appBlurb: Application, window, and top-level command shortcuts.
cat.workspacesBlurb: Workspaces live in the sidebar. Each workspace has its own set of panes and surfaces.
cat.surfacesBlurb: Surfaces are tabs inside a pane.
cat.canvasBlurb: A freeform 2D layout mode where panes float on an infinite, pannable canvas. Alignment and distribution commands are available from the command palette.

api.title: CLI Reference
api.metaDescription: cmux CLI and Unix socket API reference. Workspace management, split panes, input control, notifications, sidebar metadata, environment variables, and detection methods.
api.intro: cmux provides both a CLI tool and a Unix socket for programmatic control. Every command is available through both interfaces.
api.socketOverride: Override with the CMUX_SOCKET_PATH environment variable. Send one newline-terminated JSON request per call:
api.socketCallout: JSON socket requests must use method and params. Legacy v1 JSON payloads such as <legacy>'{'"command":"..."'}'</legacy> are not supported.
api.cmuxOnlyMode: Only processes spawned inside cmux terminals can connect.
api.allowAllMode: Allow any local process to connect (no ancestry check).
api.accessCallout: On shared machines, use Off or cmux processes only.
api.idFormat: Control identifier format in JSON output
api.newSplitDesc: Create a new split pane. Directions: left, right, up, down.
api.listSurfacesDesc: List all surfaces in the current workspace.
api.sendDesc: Send text input to the focused terminal.
api.sendKeyDesc: Send a key press. Keys: enter, tab, escape, backspace, delete, up, down, left, right.
api.sendSurfaceDesc: Send text to a specific surface.
api.sendKeySurfaceDesc: Send a key press to a specific surface.
api.sidebarMetadataDesc: Set status pills, progress bars, and log entries in the sidebar for any workspace. Useful for build scripts, CI integrations, and AI coding agents that want to surface state at a glance.
api.setStatusDesc: Set a sidebar status pill. Use a unique key so different tools can manage their own entries.
api.clearStatusDesc: Remove a sidebar status entry by key.
api.listStatusDesc: List all sidebar status entries for a workspace.
api.setProgressDesc: Set a progress bar in the sidebar (0.0 to 1.0).
api.logDesc: Append a log entry to the sidebar. Levels: info, progress, success, warning, error.
api.sidebarStateDesc: Dump all sidebar metadata (cwd, git branch, ports, status, progress, logs).
api.pingDesc: Check if cmux is running and responsive.
api.capabilitiesDesc: List available socket methods and current access mode.
api.socketPathDesc: Override the socket path used by CLI and integrations
api.socketModeDesc: Override access mode (cmuxOnly, allowAll, off). Also accepts cmux-only/cmux_only and allow-all/allow_all
api.envCallout: Legacy CMUX_SOCKET_MODE values full and notifications are still accepted for compatibility.

notifications.title: Notifications
notifications.metaDescription: Send desktop notifications from AI agents and scripts in cmux. CLI, OSC 99/777 escape sequences, and Claude Code hooks integration.
notifications.intro: cmux supports desktop notifications, allowing AI agents and scripts to alert you when they need attention.
notifications.received: Received: notification appears in panel, desktop alert fires (if not suppressed)
notifications.unread: Unread: badge shown on workspace tab
notifications.read: Read: cleared when you view that workspace
notifications.suppressItem2: The specific workspace sending the notification is active
notifications.notificationPanelDesc: Press <openShortcut>⌘⇧I</openShortcut> to open the notification panel. Click a notification to jump to that workspace. Press <jumpShortcut>⌘⇧U</jumpShortcut> to jump directly to the workspace with the most recent unread notification.
notifications.customCommandDesc: Run a shell command every time a notification is scheduled. Set it in Settings > App > Notification Command. The command runs via /bin/sh -c with these environment variables:
notifications.customCommandNote: The command runs independently of the system sound picker. Set the picker to "None" to use only the custom command, or keep both for a system sound plus a custom action.
notifications.osc777Desc: The RXVT protocol uses a fixed format with title and body:
notifications.osc99Desc: The Kitty protocol supports subtitles and notification IDs:
notifications.envTitle: Notification title (workspace name or app name)
notifications.comparisonCallout: Use OSC 777 for simple notifications. Use OSC 99 when you need subtitles or notification IDs. Use the CLI (cmux notify) for the easiest integration.
notifications.claudeCodeHooksDesc: cmux integrates with <link>Claude Code</link> via hooks to notify you when tasks complete.
notifications.restartNote: Restart Claude Code to apply the hooks.
notifications.copilotCliHooksDesc: Copilot CLI supports <link>hooks</link> that run shell commands at lifecycle events like prompt submission, agent stop, and errors.
notifications.copilotCliRepoHooks: For repo-level hooks, create a .github/hooks/notify.json file with the same structure:
notifications.tmuxDesc: If using tmux inside cmux, enable passthrough:
notifications.hooksIntro: <config>cmux.json</config> can define notification hooks that receive every notification policy as JSON on stdin. Each hook returns JSON on stdout. Hooks are off by default; cmux only runs them when <hooksMode>notifications.hooks</hooksMode> contains at least one enabled hook. cmux applies the returned notification text and effects, so hooks can filter banners, keep or skip sidebar history, run sounds, or stop later hooks.
notifications.hooksDetails: Hooks are inherited from global <globalConfig>~/.config/cmux/cmux.json</globalConfig> and project <projectConfig>.cmux/cmux.json</projectConfig> files from parent directories to the current workspace. Project hooks use the same trust prompt as other project <config>cmux.json</config> commands before they run. Feed approval banners also pass through these hooks; disabling <desktop>desktop</desktop> suppresses the native banner while keeping the Feed item available in cmux. Set <hooksMode>notifications.hooksMode</hooksMode> to <replace>replace</replace> in a project config to ignore inherited hooks. If a hook fails, times out, or returns invalid JSON, cmux uses the default notification behavior and posts a hook failure alert.

browserAutomation.title: Browser Automation
browserAutomation.metaDescription: cmux browser command reference for navigation, DOM interaction, waiting, inspection, JavaScript evaluation, tabs, dialogs, frames, downloads, and browser state.
browserAutomation.intro: The cmux browser command group provides browser automation against cmux browser surfaces. Use it to navigate, interact with DOM elements, inspect page state, evaluate JavaScript, and manage browser session data.
browserAutomation.targetingDesc: Most subcommands require a target surface. You can pass it positionally or with --surface.
browserAutomation.waitingDesc: Use wait to block until selectors, text, URL fragments, load state, or a JavaScript condition is satisfied.
browserAutomation.domDesc: Mutating actions support --snapshot-after for fast verification in scripts.
browserAutomation.inspectionDesc: Use structured getters for scripts and snapshots/screenshots for human review.
browserAutomation.stateDesc: Session data commands cover cookies, local/session storage, and full browser state snapshots.
browserAutomation.tabsDesc: Browser tab operations map to browser surfaces in the active browser tab group.
browserAutomation.patternForm: Fill a form and verify success text

skills.title: Skills
skills.metaDescription: Install and use cmux agent skills for core CLI control, workspace automation, settings, customization, diagnostics, browser automation, and markdown panels.
skills.intro: cmux ships skills that teach coding agents how to use cmux CLI control, current-workspace automation, settings, customization, diagnostics, browser surfaces, and markdown panels.
skills.installIntro: Use Vercel's skills CLI for the standard multi-agent install flow, or use skills.sh when you only need the Codex skills directory.
skills.installDestination: By default, skills.sh installs into ~/.codex/skills, or $CODEX_HOME/skills when CODEX_HOME is set. Pass --dest when your agent reads skills from another directory.
skills.localInstallIntro: From a cloned cmux checkout, the script uses the local skills directory.
skills.pinRefIntro: Use --ref to install from a specific branch, tag, or commit when the script downloads from GitHub.
skills.includedIntro: Each skill has a SKILL.md file, optional references, and an OpenAI metadata file under agents/openai.yaml.
skills.cmuxDescription: Controls windows, workspaces, panes, surfaces, focus, moves, reorder, and routing with the cmux CLI.
skills.cmuxUse: Use it when an agent needs deterministic placement or navigation in a multi-pane cmux layout.
skills.workspaceDescription: Keeps automation scoped to the current caller workspace, caller surface, panes, sidebar metadata, and socket context.
skills.workspaceUse: Use it when an agent should add panes, send input, inspect state, or open helper surfaces without disrupting another workspace.
skills.settingsDescription: Inspects, edits, validates, and opens ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json with a bundled helper script.
skills.settingsUse: Use it when changing appearance, sidebar, notification, browser, automation, or shortcut settings by JSON path.
skills.customizationDescription: Customizes cmux.json actions, plus-button behavior, tab bar buttons, workspace layouts, Dock controls, Feed hooks, sidebar settings, Command Palette entries, shortcuts, and Ghostty-owned terminal preferences.
skills.customizationUse: Use it to make cmux open a user's worktrees, multiple checkouts, SSH sessions, dev tools, or project layout from the exact UI entrypoints they want.
skills.diagnosticsDescription: Runs support-safe checks for cmux CLI health, socket access, hooks, session restore, settings, and agent binaries.
skills.diagnosticsUse: Use it when notifications, hooks, restore, or automation are not behaving as expected.
skills.browserDescription: Automates cmux webview surfaces with snapshot refs, DOM actions, waits, screenshots, and session state.
skills.browserUse: Use it for browser tasks that should run inside cmux instead of a separate browser automation tool.
skills.markdownDescription: Opens markdown files in a formatted cmux panel with live reload.
skills.markdownUse: Use it to show plans, docs, notes, and task lists beside the terminal while work is happening.

ssh.title: SSH
ssh.metaDescription: Connect to remote machines with cmux ssh. Browser panes route through the remote network, images drag-and-drop via scp, and agent notifications come home.
ssh.intro: cmux ssh creates a workspace for a remote machine. Browser panes route through the remote network, files drag-and-drop via scp, coding agents send notifications to your local sidebar, and sessions reconnect on drops.
ssh.usageDesc: cmux ssh reads your ~/.ssh/config for host aliases, identity files, and proxy settings. All flags mirror their ssh equivalents.
ssh.flagNoFocus: Create the workspace without switching to it
ssh.deepLinksDesc: Use cmux SSH deep links when a website or tool wants to offer an Open in cmux button. The link opens cmux, shows a confirmation prompt, then runs the equivalent cmux ssh command after the user confirms.
ssh.deepLinksWebFallbackDesc: Use the cmux.com fallback URL for website buttons. It opens the native link and shows the download if cmux is not installed.
ssh.deepLinksPromptRulesDesc: Prompt and rules buttons use the same fallback shape. Commas, colons, and literal plus signs are preserved when they are URL encoded:
ssh.deepLinksIconDesc: Use the SVG icon for dashboard buttons, or the PNG logo when a raster image is required:
ssh.deepLinksButtonDesc: Build the fallback URL with URLSearchParams so titles, host aliases, and user names are encoded safely:
ssh.deepLinkUser: Optional SSH user. cmux combines it with host as user@host.
ssh.deepLinkTitle: Optional workspace title. Use only one of title or name.
ssh.deepLinkConnectTimeout: Optional ConnectTimeout value in seconds, 1 through 600.
ssh.deepLinkServerAliveInterval: Optional ServerAliveInterval value in seconds, 1 through 3600.
ssh.deepLinkHostKeyPolicy: Optional StrictHostKeyChecking policy: accept-new, ask, strict, or yes.
ssh.deepLinkNoFocus: Optional boolean. true creates the workspace without switching to it.
ssh.deepLinksSchemeDesc: Use cmux:// for the stable app, cmux-nightly:// for Nightly, and cmux-dev:// for Debug or tagged dev builds.
ssh.deepLinksSecurityDesc: External links cannot pass identity files, raw ssh options, commands, ProxyCommand, or forwarding rules. Put keys, ProxyJump, HostName, and advanced options in ~/.ssh/config instead. cmux displays the command preview and requires the user to trust the SSH target before connecting.
ssh.browserDesc: Browser panes in a remote workspace route all HTTP and WebSocket traffic through the remote machine's network. Type localhost:3000 and you're looking at the dev server running on the remote box. No -L flags, no manual port forwarding. Each remote workspace gets an isolated cookie store so sessions are scoped per-connection.
ssh.dragDropDesc: Drag an image or file into a remote terminal and cmux uploads it via scp through the existing SSH connection. cmux detects the foreground SSH process by TTY and routes the upload through ControlMaster multiplexing.
ssh.notificationsDesc: Processes on the remote machine can run cmux commands that execute on your local instance. When a coding agent calls cmux notify on the remote box, the notification appears in your local sidebar. The blue ring lights up on the workspace tab. Cmd+Shift+U jumps to it. Notification spam from flaky connections is suppressed with a per-host cooldown.
ssh.agentsDesc: cmux claude-teams and cmux omo both work inside SSH sessions. The Go relay daemon on the remote host handles the same tmux-compat translation that the local Swift CLI does. Teammate agents spawn as native cmux splits on your local machine while computation runs on the remote box.
ssh.reconnectDesc: When the connection drops, cmux reconnects with exponential backoff (3s, 6s, 12s, up to 60s). The remote session persists and cmux reattaches on reconnect, resizing with smallest-screen-wins semantics. Default keepalive options (ServerAliveInterval=20, ServerAliveCountMax=2) are injected unless your config already sets them.
ssh.daemonDesc: On first connect, cmux probes the remote host (uname -s, uname -m) and uploads a versioned cmuxd-remote binary. The binary speaks JSON-RPC over stdio and handles three things:
ssh.daemonProxyHow: SOCKS5 and HTTP CONNECT over the daemon's stdio channel
ssh.daemonRelayHow: Reverse TCP tunnel with HMAC-SHA256 auth so remote processes can call cmux commands locally
ssh.daemonSessionHow: Persists sessions across reconnects, coordinates PTY resize across multiple attachments
ssh.daemonPath: The daemon binary is stored at ~/.cmux/bin/cmuxd-remote/<version>/<os>-<arch>/cmuxd-remote on the remote host and verified against a SHA-256 manifest embedded in the app.

claudeCodeTeams.metaDescription: Run Claude Code with agent teams inside cmux. Teammate agents spawn as native cmux splits instead of tmux panes.

claudeCodeTeams.shimStep2: Sets TMUX and TMUX_PANE environment variables to simulate a tmux session
claudeCodeTeams.shimStep3: Prepends the shim directory to PATH so Claude finds the shim before real tmux
claudeCodeTeams.shimStep4: Enables CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1 and sets teammate mode to auto

claudeCodeTeams.envTmuxPane: Fake tmux pane identifier mapped to the current cmux pane
claudeCodeTeams.envTeams: Enables Claude Code agent teams feature
claudeCodeTeams.envSocket: Path to the cmux control socket for the shim to connect to
claudeCodeTeams.dirShim: Contains the tmux shim script that translates tmux commands to cmux API calls
claudeCodeTeams.dirStore: Persistent storage for tmux-compat buffers and hooks
claudeCodeTeams.tmuxCommandsDesc: The shim translates these tmux commands into cmux operations:
claudeCodeTeams.mapSendText: sends text to a cmux surface

ohMyOpenCode.whatYouGet2: Auto-layout management: agents are arranged in a grid (main-vertical by default) and resized as agents come and go
ohMyOpenCode.whatYouGet3: Idle agents are automatically cleaned up after 3 consecutive idle polls with no new messages

ohMyOpenCode.firstRunStep1: Creates a shadow config at ~/.cmuxterm/omo-config/ with oh-my-opencode registered in the plugin array
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunStep2: Installs the oh-my-opencode npm package using bun or npm if not already present
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunStep3: Symlinks node_modules, package.json, and plugin config from your original ~/.config/opencode/ directory
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunStep4: Enables tmux mode in the oh-my-opencode config (tmux.enabled defaults to false, cmux omo turns it on)
ohMyOpenCode.firstRunSafe: Your original ~/.config/opencode/ config is never modified. Running plain opencode works the same as before.
