ohMyOpenCode.shimStep1: Creates a tmux shim at ~/.cmuxterm/omo-bin/tmux that redirects to cmux __tmux-compat
ohMyOpenCode.shimStep2: Sets TMUX and TMUX_PANE to simulate a tmux session
ohMyOpenCode.shimStep3: Enables tmux.enabled in the oh-my-opencode config (required for visual pane spawning)
ohMyOpenCode.shimStep4: Points OPENCODE_CONFIG_DIR at the shadow config
ohMyOpenCode.shimStep5: Prepends the shim directory to PATH and execs into opencode
ohMyOpenCode.dirShadow: Shadow OpenCode config with oh-my-opencode plugin registered and tmux enabled (symlinks to your original config)
ohMyOpenCode.dirStore: Persistent storage for tmux-compat buffers and hooks
ohMyOpenCode.shadowConfigDesc: cmux omo uses a shadow config directory so your original OpenCode setup is unaffected:
ohMyOpenCode.shadowStep1: Copies your ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json with oh-my-opencode added to the plugin array
ohMyOpenCode.shadowStep2: Symlinks node_modules, package.json, and bun.lock from the original directory
ohMyOpenCode.shadowStep3: Writes oh-my-opencode.json with tmux.enabled set to true
ohMyOpenCode.shadowStep4: Sets OPENCODE_CONFIG_DIR to the shadow directory before launching opencode

ohMyOpenCode.envTmuxPane: Fake tmux pane identifier mapped to the current cmux pane

dock.metaDescription: Add custom terminal UI controls to the cmux right sidebar with Dock JSON.
dock.title: Dock
dock.intro: Dock lets you add terminal UI controls to the right sidebar. Each control is a command from JSON, rendered in its own Ghostty-backed terminal section. Use it for feeds, logs, queues, git status, dev servers, or any TUI your team wants near every workspace.
dock.configIntro: cmux looks for Dock configuration in this order:
dock.projectConfig: for the current repo, nearest parent project, and nested project directories.
dock.globalConfig: for your personal default Dock or when there is no repo.
dock.precedenceCallout: If a project and global config both exist, the project config wins. Nested project configs apply to their directory tree. If no project config exists, Dock uses the global config. If neither file exists, Dock opens empty.
dock.trustCallout: Project Dock configs can start commands. cmux asks you to trust a project config before launching its controls.
dock.exampleIntro: A Dock file is a JSON object with a controls array. Commit project controls to .cmux/dock.json when you want teammates to share the same Dock. Replace example commands with tools your repo actually has.
dock.fieldId: Stable unique identifier for the control. Keep this short and do not reuse it for another command.
dock.fieldTitle: Label shown in the Dock header.
dock.fieldCommand: Command to run in the Dock terminal. It starts inside your login shell.
dock.fieldCwd: Optional working directory. Relative paths resolve from the project root for project configs, or from your home directory for global configs.
dock.fieldHeight: Optional requested terminal height in points. Controls without a height share the remaining space.
dock.fieldEnv: Optional environment variables passed only to that control.
dock.sharingIntro: Dock is designed to be shared with source control when the commands belong to the repo.
dock.sharingProject: Put repo-specific controls in .cmux/dock.json and commit the file.
dock.sharingGlobal: Put personal controls in ~/.config/cmux/dock.json, especially outside a repo, and keep that file out of shared source control.
dock.sharingSecrets: Do not put secrets in dock.json. Read secrets from your shell, a local env file, or your existing dev tooling.
dock.agentPromptTitle: Ask an agent to set it up
dock.agentPromptIntro: Use this prompt when you want a coding agent to create Dock controls. It tells the agent to run `cmux docs dock`, inspect the project, and ask before guessing.
dock.agentPrompt:
Set up cmux Dock controls for the current context.

First, learn the feature before editing:
1. Run `cmux docs dock` if the cmux CLI is available. If it is not, read https://cmux.com/docs/dock.
2. Inspect the repository or current directory to understand the project type, scripts, package manager, dev servers, logs, task runners, test commands, and any existing TUI tools.
3. If the desired Dock is ambiguous, ask the user what they want monitored or controlled before writing files.

Dock is cmux's right-sidebar terminal control area. A Dock config is JSON with a top-level `controls` array. Each control runs a command in its own Ghostty-backed terminal section using the user's login shell. Controls are useful for project dashboards, git/status views, dev server or build status, test watchers, log tails, queues, local services, or a custom TUI such as `cmux feed tui --opentui` when that feed is useful.

Choose where to write the config:
- In a repository or project directory, create or edit `.cmux/dock.json` so teammates can share it.
- For a personal default outside a repo, create or edit `~/.config/cmux/dock.json`.
- If both exist, project `.cmux/dock.json` is more specific for that project. Nested project configs apply to that directory tree; use the nearest relevant project config instead of writing unrelated controls globally.
- If there is no repo and no clear project root, use the global config only after confirming the user wants a personal Dock.

Schema:
{
  "controls": [
    {
      "id": "short-stable-id",
      "title": "Human label",
      "command": "safe command to run",
      "cwd": "optional/path",
      "height": 220,
      "env": { "NAME": "value" }
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Keep ids stable, lowercase, and unique.
- Use `cwd` for subdirectories; relative paths resolve from the config base.
- Use `height` only when a control needs a fixed amount of vertical space.
- Use `env` only for non-secret values needed by one control.
- Do not put secrets, tokens, or machine-specific private paths in a shared project config.
- Prefer commands that are safe to start repeatedly and make sense in a terminal.
- Do not invent unavailable scripts. Read package files, Makefiles, Procfiles, README docs, config files, and existing tooling first.
- Keep shared project Docks portable for teammates. Put personal or machine-specific controls in the global Dock.

Deliverable:
- Create or update the appropriate dock.json.
- Preserve existing useful controls unless the user asked to replace them.
- Validate that the JSON parses.
- Summarize what each control does and any commands the user should review before trusting the Dock config.

textBox.title: TextBox (Beta)
textBox.metaDescription: Configure the beta cmux TextBox input for new terminal tabs, splits, and workspaces.
textBox.intro: TextBox is cmux's rich terminal input surface for composing prompts before sending them to a terminal.
textBox.betaCallout: TextBox is a beta feature. Its defaults and behavior may change while it is being tested.
textBox.defaultsDesc: The TextBox settings live in Settings > TextBox:
textBox.showSetting: Show TextBox on New Terminals opens new workspaces, terminal tabs, and terminal splits with the TextBox visible while keeping focus in the terminal.
textBox.focusSetting: Focus TextBox on New Terminals puts keyboard focus in the TextBox. Focusing also shows the TextBox.
textBox.maxLinesSetting: TextBox Max Lines caps the TextBox height before its content scrolls.
textBox.configDesc: Set the same defaults in ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json under terminal:
textBox.focusNote: Restored sessions keep their saved TextBox visibility and focus state. These defaults apply only to newly created terminal surfaces.

wallOfLove.title: Wall of Love
wallOfLove.description: What people are saying about cmux.
wallOfLove.metaDescription: What people are saying about cmux, the terminal built for multitasking.

testimonials.mitchellh: Another day another libghostty-based project, this time a macOS terminal with vertical tabs, better organization/notifications, embedded/scriptable browser specifically targeted towards people who use a ton of terminal-based agentic workflows.
testimonials.steipete: I'm late to the party, but cmux is great. Current split: Codex Mac app for knowledge work, learning, reading; cmux + Codex CLI for coding.
testimonials.schrockn: This is exactly the product I've been looking for. After two hours this am I've in love.
testimonials.egrefen: I've been using this all weekend and it's amazing.
testimonials.max4c: this has been my favorite tool for past two weeks
testimonials.lucas_flatwhite: It's not too late. I use Ghostty a lot, and cmux is love too. Vertical tabs, notification rings, built-in browser, split panes, GPU acceleration... there are so many real benefits!
testimonials.yamadashy: I've used cmux for a while and I like it. It feels less like 'this is amazing' and more like 'yes, this is it.' It also pairs well with k1Low/mo.
testimonials.minixalpha: I also use cmux as my main terminal and recommend it to coworkers. The notifications, workspaces, and shortcuts improve efficiency when running multiple jobs in parallel. Even though cmux is a bit ugly, its functionality makes it indispensable.
testimonials.juan_barbat: I had an issue with the browser, but cmux is indispensable in my day to day.
testimonials.yousefrol: My suggestion is just use cmux. It solved almost every terminal problem for me.
testimonials.tobiasgloeckler: I just installed cmux. I had been using Ghostty, but cmux is even better suited for AI agents and coding.
testimonials.wescld: Man, I don't know how I lived so long without cmux.
testimonials.jinchenma_ai: cmux is the terminal tool I use most lately. It's open source and free. It has basically replaced iTerm2 for me and perfectly solves the multi-terminal window layout problem.
testimonials.zvasil: I moved to it from Warp a while ago and it felt like switching to a rocket. It's native macOS Swift and being developed super actively.
testimonials.immazzystar: A tool I've liked using recently: cmux. I no longer have to switch terminal windows constantly.
testimonials.blitz_zidan: cmux is my favorite terminal lately. Personally, I think it's really good for running multiple terminals.
testimonials.asaza: cmux looks so good it might finally be time to say goodbye to VSCode
testimonials.ssarisen: If you need to work with multiple terminals, you should definitely try cmux. When a terminal waits for your input, it sends an automatic notification.
testimonials.johnthedebs: Hey, this looks seriously awesome. Love the ideas here, specifically: the programmability, layered UI, browser w/ api. Looking forward to giving this a spin. Also want to add that I really appreciate Mitchell Hashimoto creating libghostty; it feels like an exciting time to be a terminal user.
testimonials.joeriddles: Vertical tabs in my terminal! I never thought of that before. I use and love Firefox vertical tabs.
testimonials.dchu17: Gave this a run and it was pretty intuitive. Good work!
testimonials.afruth: I like it, ran it in the past day on three parallel projects each with several worktrees. Having this paired with lazygit and yazi / nvim made me a bit more productive than usual without having to chase multiple ghostty / iTerm instances. Also feels more natural than tmux.
testimonials.northprint: Tried cmux since it looked good — it's good
testimonials.scottw: This has been such a useful find. I can't recommend it enough.
testimonials.johnblythe: grabbed this over the weekend and loved it. been waiting for something like this.
testimonials.bchris91: This is exactly what I've wanted. Amazing job thank you!
testimonials.connorelsea: Been using this for a week and it's fantastic. Vert tab for each WIP task. Inside, claudes on one side and browser with PR and resources on the other, switch between tasks and stay organized. Mix that with skills to have Claude watch CI recursively, etc. feeling enlightened tbh
testimonials.tonkotsuboy: I switched from Warp to Ghostty at the start of the year, but now I've switched to cmux. The vertical tabs are convenient, and I appreciate getting notified when Claude Code tasks finish. It's Ghostty-based so the blazing fast performance carries over. Branch display and completions I set up in Ghostty still work too.

nightly.title: cmux NIGHTLY
nightly.subtitle: Bleeding-edge builds from main
nightly.metaDescription: Download cmux NIGHTLY, a separate app built automatically from the latest main commit. Runs alongside the stable version with its own auto-updates.
nightly.description: cmux NIGHTLY is built automatically from the latest commit on main. It has its own bundle ID, so it runs alongside the stable version without conflicts. Use it to test new features before they ship.
nightly.warning: Nightly builds may contain bugs or incomplete features. If something breaks, report it on <githubLink>GitHub</githubLink> or in <discordLink>#nightly-bugs on Discord</discordLink>, and switch back to the stable release.
